texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We develop an operator-theoretic framework for finite-dimensional, regime-dependent Volterra equations with completely monotone memory kernels, dissipative network coupling, and Hawkestype self-excitation. For each fixed regime we construct the associated Volterra resolvent family and prove global well-posedness, continuity across regime switches, and explicit a priori bounds. The main stability result is sharp in the commuting case: after simultaneous diagonalization of the network Laplacian and the excitation operator, each mode obeys a scalar characteristic equation, and global asymptotic stability holds exactly when every modal branching ratio lies below the intensity damping threshold. We also give a norm-based sufficient condition for noncommuting operators and a Perron-Frobenius spectral criterion for nonnegative intensity blocks, showing when norm estimates are conservative. Beyond mean stability, we prove a pathwise finite-range power law for burst amplitudes generated by residence in a Hurwitz but nonnormal regime: under a cone-alignment event, the survival exponent is the ratio of the regime exit rate to a cone-corrected finite-time growth rate bounded above by the logarithmic norm of a fixed Markovian realization in the chosen Euclidean metric. A complementary idealized-feedback result shows how a logarithmic-norm contraction caps the amplification band. Finally, we derive the deterministic intensity block as a mean-field limit of a relaxing long-memory Hawkes system with regimes. Numerical experiments on modal equations, a small-world network, and a switched nonnormal ODE validate the sharp threshold and the finite-range amplification mechanism without using the closed-form tail formula as input.
texts.text: We develop an operator-theoretic framework for finite-dimensional, regime-dependent Volterra equations with completely monotone memory kernels, dissipative network coupling, and Hawkestype self-excitation. For each fixed regime we construct the associated Volterra resolvent family and prove global well-posedness, continuity across regime switches, and explicit a priori bounds. The main stability result is sharp in the commuting case: after simultaneous diagonalization of the network Laplacian and the excitation operator, each mode obeys a scalar characteristic equation, and global asymptotic stability holds exactly when every modal branching ratio lies below the intensity damping threshold. We also give a norm-based sufficient condition for noncommuting operators and a Perron-Frobenius spectral criterion for nonnegative intensity blocks, showing when norm estimates are conservative. Beyond mean stability, we prove a pathwise finite-range power law for burst amplitudes generated by residence in a Hurwitz but nonnormal regime: under a cone-alignment event, the survival exponent is the ratio of the regime exit rate to a cone-corrected finite-time growth rate bounded above by the logarithmic norm of a fixed Markovian realization in the chosen Euclidean metric. A complementary idealized-feedback result shows how a logarithmic-norm contraction caps the amplification band. Finally, we derive the deterministic intensity block as a mean-field limit of a relaxing long-memory Hawkes system with regimes. Numerical experiments on modal equations, a small-world network, and a switched nonnormal ODE validate the sharp threshold and the finite-range amplification mechanism without using the closed-form tail formula as input.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Equation (1) is a common abstraction of several models in which memory, network geometry, self-excitation, and latent regime modulation act simultaneously. The completely monotone kernel g z models long-range (in particular, tempered power-law and fractional) memory; the operator B models diffusion or consensus dynamics on the network; the convolution term models Hawkes-type self-excitation whose strength is regime-dependent; and the process Z ( t ) models slow switches of the operative dynamical regime, in the spirit of stochastic systems with Markovian switching [16]. Each ingredient is individually classical. Their interaction is not, and it is the source of the phenomena we analyze.

texts.orig: 1.2 Theoretical gaps and contributions
texts.text: 1.2 Theoretical gaps and contributions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The classical theory of scalar Volterra equations with completely monotone kernels is due to Prüss and collaborators [17], with the fractional case treated through the resolvent-family calculus of Bazhlekova [1], the general Volterra theory of Gripenberg, Londen, and Staffans [10], and the Bernstein function machinery of Schilling, Song, and Vondraček [18]. Network diffusion and Laplacian spectral methods are standard [3]. Self-exciting point processes and their stability through the branching ratio go back to Hawkes [11, 12], with stability theory due to Brémaud and Massoulié [2] and the general framework of Daley and Vere-Jones [7], and the scaling-limit theory of nearly unstable Hawkes processes-which produces rough fractional limits-is due to Jaisson and Rosenbaum [13, 14]. Heavy tails generated by multiplicative feedback are classical through the Kesten-Goldie theory [15, 9].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Well-posedness and resolvent calculus (Section 3). For each fixed regime we construct a strongly continuous Volterra resolvent family {R z ( t ) } t ≥ 0 ⊂ L ( X ) for (1), prove existence and uniqueness of mild and strong solutions, establish sharp finite-horizon and uniform-in-time a priori bounds controlled by a fractional branching ratio ρ z , and prove continuity of solutions across regime switches.
texts.text: Well-posedness and resolvent calculus (Section 3). For each fixed regime we construct a strongly continuous Volterra resolvent family {R z ( t ) } t ≥ 0 ⊂ L ( X ) for (1), prove existence and uniqueness of mild and strong solutions, establish sharp finite-horizon and uniform-in-time a priori bounds controlled by a fractional branching ratio ρ z , and prove continuity of solutions across regime switches.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. A sharp modal stability criterion (Section 4). Under simultaneous diagonalization of A z and L G (Assumption 2), we prove that the homogeneous evolution is globally asymptotically stable if and only if the modal fractional branching ratio satisfies ρ z,j < c λ for every Laplacian mode j (Theorem 4.1), a trichotomy with a critical case governed by the low-frequency kernel asymptotics. We give a sharp norm-based sufficient condition in the noncommuting case (Proposition 4.4) and prove topology-independence under scalar excitation (Corollary 4.6).

We give a sharp norm-based sufficient condition in the noncommuting case (Proposition 4.4) and prove topology-independence under scalar excitation (Corollary 4.6).

We isolate a memory-induced mechanism by which regime residence produces a power-law burst law over a finite band of magnitudes, generated by nonnormal transient growth, even when every regime operator is Hurwitz. texts.orig: Three relations deserve emphasis, because they delimit what is and is not new.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, our resolvent construction extends the scalar completely monotone Volterra theory [17, 1] to block-structured, regime-dependent, operator-valued kernels on a product Hilbert space; the novelty is the modal spectral sharpness Theorem 4.1 and its behavior across switches, not the existence theory in isolation.
texts.text: First, our resolvent construction extends the scalar completely monotone Volterra theory [17, 1] to block-structured, regime-dependent, operator-valued kernels on a product Hilbert space; the novelty is the modal spectral sharpness Theorem 4.1 and its behavior across switches, not the existence theory in isolation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Second, the hydrodynamic limit is in the lineage of Jaisson and Rosenbaum [13, 14], but those works prove weak convergence in distribution of a single-regime rescaled process to a limit law and do not provide an operator stability criterion. Our Theorem 6.1 is a convergence theorem feeding the sharp spectral threshold of Theorem 4.1, across regimes.
texts.text: Second, the hydrodynamic limit is in the lineage of Jaisson and Rosenbaum [13, 14], but those works prove weak convergence in distribution of a single-regime rescaled process to a limit law and do not provide an operator stability criterion. Our Theorem 6.1 is a convergence theorem feeding the sharp spectral threshold of Theorem 4.1, across regimes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Third, the quenched tail of Theorem 5.3 is not a large-deviation statement. Its exponent is a logarithmic-norm quantity of the fixed regime realization, rather than the minimizer of an action functional, and it can be positive even when the operator is Hurwitz, where no large-deviation instability exists. This is why the formulation is operator-theoretic: after a realization and inner product are fixed, the governing object is the logarithmic norm of the lifted generator. We expand on this in Remark 5.6.

texts.orig: Remark 2.1 (Scope of the setting) . We work throughout in the finite-dimensional space X = R n × R n , so every operator below is a bounded matrix with full domain, and all spectral statements refer to matrix spectra. We use resolvent-family language because it is the natural calculus for the nonlocal (Volterra) part of (1), and because every constant we track is dimension-free-depending only on the operator norms, the kernel mass, and the dissipation c ∗ , not on n . The extension to an abstract infinite-dimensional node Hilbert space with a sectorial generator B is therefore natural but requires the operator-semigroup machinery of [17]; we state it as future work and do not claim it here, so as to keep every proof self-contained and finite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3.1 (Deterministic paths versus stochastic regimes) . Assumption 1 treats Z as a given piecewise-constant signal. This is the framework for well-posedness, resolvent bounds, and the fixed-regime spectral criteria: all estimates are pathwise and can be concatenated over the observed switch times. The probabilistic statements in Section 5 add an extra assumption on the law of the residence time in the unfavorable regime, typically an exponential residence time with rate λ U . Thus no stochastic assumption on Z is used until the quenched-tail theorem; before that point the theory is conditional on a prescribed switching path.

On [ t k -1 , t k ] , U is continuous by Theorem 3.2 applied to regime Z ( t k -1 ) with the memory integral ∫ t 0 g ( t -s ) A U ( s ) d s taken over the entire history [0 , t ] ; the history is a fixed continuous datum on this interval, so the variation-of-constants map is still a contraction and yields a continuous solution up to and including t k . At t k the regime switches to Z ( t k ) ; the new evolution starts from U ( t k ) and again includes the full past in its memory term. Since the representation (12) for the new regime satisfies R Z ( t k ) (0) = I and the forcing/memory integrals vanish as t ↓ t k , we get lim t ↓ t k U ( t ) = U ( t k ) = lim t ↑ t k U ( t ) . The memory term is continuous across t k because g z ∈ L 1 and the integrand is bounded, so no compatibility condition is needed.

texts.orig: Remark 3.7 . Lemma 3.6 is what makes (1) a genuine piecewise-deterministic evolution with memory: switching deforms the operator pair instantaneously but the state and its entire history vary continuously. This is the analytic underpinning of the regime-dependent spectral 'deformation' studied next, and it is exactly the structure absent from single-regime Volterra/Hawkes scaling-limit theory.

texts.orig: (i) (Subcritical) If ρ z,j < c λ , then D z,j ( ξ ) = 0 for all Re ξ ≥ 0 , and the mode is stable. Under (C1) the decay is exponential: if the intensity symbol is zero-free in a strip Re ξ ≥ -ω λ,j , then for every 0 < ω j < min { ω λ,j , c x + κℓ j } there is M j ≥ 1 such that | λ j ( t ) | ≤ M j e -ω j t ( | x j (0) | + | λ j (0) | ) . Under (C2) the decay is algebraic, governed by the low-frequency expansion: | λ j ( t ) | = O ( t -β -1 ) as t →∞ .

If some a z,j < 0 , the quantity | a z,j | G z < c λ remains sufficient for stability (the bound | a z,j ̂ g z ( ξ ) | ≤ | a z,j | G z used in (i) does not require sign-definiteness), but it need not be necessary, since a negative gain is stabilizing and D z,j may have no right-half-plane zero even when | a z,j | G z ≥ c λ . texts.orig: Proof. By Theorem 4.1, each decoupled mode is asymptotically stable precisely when ρ z,j < c λ ; under (C1) the convergence is exponential, whereas under (C2) the subcritical convergence can be algebraic. Since only finitely many modes are present, the slowest stable mode dictates the global rate, so asymptotic stability holds iff the maximum modal branching ratio is below c λ . If some ρ z,j ≥ c λ the corresponding mode is critical or unstable, precluding global asymptotic stability.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Finite-range power-law amplification under regime residence
texts.text: 5 Finite-range power-law amplification under regime residence

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The stability theory of Section 4 concerns the annealed (deterministic, ensemble) evolution: when ρ max z < c λ in every visited regime, the mean dynamics is bounded. We now show that this is compatible with a quenched (pathwise) burst observable exhibiting a power-law law over a finite range of magnitudes, generated by the interaction of operator non-normality with regime residence. We are deliberately careful about the nature of the claim: because the unfavorable operator is Hurwitz, the transient amplification it produces is bounded, so the phenomenon is a fi nite-range power law-a heavy tail over a band of burst sizes set by the residence horizon-rather than a true asymptotic ( b →∞ ) heavy tail. We make this precise below. The mechanism is nonetheless genuinely nonlinear in that it is invisible to the spectral abscissa: it operates even when every regime operator is Hurwitz.

texts.orig: Remark 5.4 (Why finite-range, and why this is the honest statement) . Because A U is Hurwitz, ∥ ∥ e tA U ∥ ∥ grows transiently to a finite peak gain and then decays; a single residence can amplify a burst by at most the factor realized within the horizon T 0 , capping the power law at b max in (22). The statement (23) is thus a genuine power law over a band of magnitudes, not an asymptotic heavy tail. This distinction is essential and we do not overstate it: the phenomenon is residence-induced finite-range power-law amplification . A true asymptotic heavy tail would require either a non-Hurwitz (marginally stable) A U with sustained growth, or unbounded residence times; both lie outside the present hypotheses and are noted as extensions.

texts.orig: Remark 5.5 (How the cone-alignment probability is verified) . Assumption 5 is a structural hypothesis, not an automatic consequence of nonnormality. For a general Hurwitz nonnormal matrix, transient growth may rotate trajectories out of the cone { v ⊤ U X ≥ a ∥ X ∥} . In applications one may verify p 0 > 0 either analytically, for a specified entry distribution and forcing, or empirically by estimating the conditional frequency of residences for which the cone inequality holds on [ t 0 , t 0 + τ ] . A convenient sufficient condition in low-dimensional reductions is a positive gap in the symmetric part together with forcing and entry states concentrated near v U ; however, this sufficient condition is model specific, and the theorem only assumes the verifiable probability lower bound stated in Assumption 5.

texts.orig: Remark 5.6 (Why the exponent is logarithmic-norm based, not large-deviation) . The theorem uses the cone-corrected rate γ c U = γ U -∥ A U ∥ √ 1 -a 2 /a , with γ U = µ 2 ( A U ) computed for the fixed lifted regime operator in the chosen Euclidean coordinates. Thus the exponent is κ = λ U /γ c U ; the simpler quotient λ U /γ U is the perfectly aligned limiting scale, not the generic finite-band prediction. The distinction is important: the logarithmic norm is an instantaneous upper growth scale, while a realized trajectory may rotate away from its maximally expanding direction and therefore exhibit an effective rate below γ U . The expression contains no action functional and no small parameter, and it can be positive when A U is Hurwitz but nonnormal. This is weaker and more precise than calling γ U an invariant of the original kernel: two minimal Markovian realizations related by a non-orthogonal similarity need not have the same Euclidean logarithmic norm. What is invariant in the present theorem is the statement after the realization and inner product are fixed; the logarithmic-norm scale is then a computable upper anchor for the cone-corrected growth rate that feeds the residence-survival transform.

The idealized contraction feedback replaces, instantaneously and for the remainder of any residence in U during which the indicator S ( t ) := v ⊤ U X ( t ) / ∥ X ( t ) ∥ exceeds s 0 , the operator A U by a modified operator A ′ U with µ 2 ( A ′ U ) ≤ -σ < 0 . texts.orig: Proposition 5.9 (Per-residence amplification cap) . Assume, in addition to Definition 5.8, that the forcing and initial data are bounded ( ∥ ∥ ∥ ˜ f ∥ ∥ ∥ ∞ ≤ F 0 , ∥ X (0) ∥ ≤ X 0 ), that outside U the dynamics is globally dissipative ( µ 2 ( A z ) ≤ -c ∗ < 0 for z = U ), and that there is no accumulation of amplification across distinct residences (each residence enters with norm bounded by a fixed X 1 determined by the dissipative inter-residence decay). If every residence in U of duration exceeding a trigger time δ > 0 activates the feedback, then the per-residence amplification is capped: there is b cap < ∞ , depending only on ( γ U , δ, σ, F 0 , X 1 ) , such that no controlled residence produces a burst exceeding b cap , and consequently P ( B ′ T > b ) = 0 for b > b cap .

texts.orig: Remark 5.10 (Steeper finite-range law under delayed engagement) . If the feedback engages only after an additional delay δ ′ > 0 (the indicator must persist before actuation), the achievable growth is realized over duration δ + δ ′ rather than the full horizon T 0 . Heuristically this contracts the amplification band of Theorem 5.3 and rescales the effective exponent to κ ′ ≈ κT 0 / ( δ + δ ′ ) > κ , a steeper finite-range power law on the controlled range. We state this as a heuristic rather than a theorem: a rigorous exponent under delayed feedback requires modeling the joint law of residence durations and trigger times, which lies outside the present scope. The robust and proven content is the amplification cap of Proposition 5.9; the logarithmic-norm principle is that contracting µ 2 ( A U ) bounds the finite-range amplification.

texts.orig: Remark 6.3 . Theorem 6.1 identifies the modal branching ratio with a microscopic reproduction number. In the Perron mode of a nonnegative excitation matrix, the expected total memory-weighted offspring is r ( A z ) G z , while c λ is the linear relaxation rate of activity. Thus macroscopic modal instability is the deterministic image of microscopic supercriticality. The single-regime scaling-limit theory of Hawkes processes [13, 14] is recovered as a special case, whereas the present formulation feeds directly into the operator stability criterion of Section 4.

(A) Scalar modal Volterra dynamics with the tempered fractional kernel (32): branching ratios ρ = 0 . 65 , 1 . 00 , 1 . 25 illustrate the subcritical, critical, and supercritical regimes of Theorem 4.1; in the supercritical case the fitted growth rate agrees with the characteristic root ξ ∗ . (B) Stability diagnostics on a 120 -node Watts-Strogatz network: in the commuting case the exact modal criterion and the norm criterion coincide, while in the nonnormal noncommuting case the norm-based condition (Proposition 4.4) is far more conservative than the spectral criterion (Proposition 4.5). (C) A Hurwitz but nonnormal operator has α ( A U ) < 0 while µ 2 ( A U ) > 0 , giving transient amplification despite asymptotic stability. (D) Direct simulation of the switched ODE (40), with exponentially distributed residence times in the unfavorable regime and a dissipative safe regime after exit, produces a finite-range power-law-like survival band. The fitted slope is compared with the cone-corrected finite-band rate obtained from the realized alignment distribution and with a matrix-based diagnostic estimated from the integrated ODE response, not imposed through the closed-form residence-to-burst transformation.

texts.orig: Together these experiments validate the analytic mechanisms developed above. The scalar simulation verifies that the rightmost zero of the modal characteristic function governs the trichotomy at ρ = c λ . The network experiment shows that the modal criterion is sharp in the commuting case and that norm-based estimates may be severely conservative under nonnormal noncommuting excitation, exactly the gap that Proposition 4.5 closes. The residence experiment confirms the finite-range mechanism non-circularly: the burst law is obtained by integrating a switched ODE and only then fitting the survival band.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For completeness we record the analytic trichotomy on the smallest nontrivial network, the path graph on three nodes with Laplacian eigenvalues ℓ 1 = 0 , ℓ 2 = 1 , ℓ 3 = 3 . With scalar excitation A z = a z I (so Assumption 2 and Assumption 3 hold) and c λ = 1 , every mode has the same branching ratio ρ z = a z θ -α , and Theorem 4.1 gives: subcritical decay if a z θ -α < 1 ; algebraic relaxation t -(1 -β ) if a heavy integrable kernel of class (C2) is used at criticality a z G = c λ (for the tempered kernel of class (C1) the critical relaxation is to a constant, not algebraic-see Theorem 4.1(ii)); and exponential growth e ξ ∗ t at the rightmost root ξ ∗ > 0 of ξ +1 -a z ̂ g z ( ξ ) = 0 if a z θ -α > 1 . Because the excitation is scalar, this threshold is topology-independent (Corollary 4.6).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8 Discussion
texts.text: 8 Discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have developed a rigorous theory for regime-dependent operator-valued Volterra equations with completely monotone memory, establishing a fractional resolvent calculus, a sharp modal stability criterion, a finite-range power-law amplification mechanism driven by operator non-normality, and a hydrodynamic limit linking the deterministic intensity block to relaxing microscopic Hawkes dynamics. Three points situate the results.
texts.text: We have developed a rigorous theory for regime-dependent operator-valued Volterra equations with completely monotone memory, establishing a fractional resolvent calculus, a sharp modal stability criterion, a finite-range power-law amplification mechanism driven by operator non-normality, and a hydrodynamic limit linking the deterministic intensity block to relaxing microscopic Hawkes dynamics. Three points situate the results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The modal criterion of Theorem 4.1 is sharp-necessary and sufficient under simultaneous diagonalization-and isolates the network modes responsible for instability, recovering the normbased sufficient condition as a corollary and exhibiting topology-independent criticality under scalar excitation. The finite-range amplification of Theorem 5.3 is, to our knowledge, a new mechanism: it is anchored by the logarithmic norm of a Hurwitz but non-normal operator under regime residence, while the actual exponent uses the cone-corrected finite-band rate realized by aligned trajectories rather than a large-deviation rate functional (Remark 5.6); its amplification is capped by logarithmic-norm contraction (Proposition 5.9). The hydrodynamic limit of Theorem 6.1 makes the branching-ratio terminology precise and ties macroscopic spectral instability to microscopic explosion.
texts.text: The modal criterion of Theorem 4.1 is sharp-necessary and sufficient under simultaneous diagonalization-and isolates the network modes responsible for instability, recovering the normbased sufficient condition as a corollary and exhibiting topology-independent criticality under scalar excitation. The finite-range amplification of Theorem 5.3 is, to our knowledge, a new mechanism: it is anchored by the logarithmic norm of a Hurwitz but non-normal operator under regime residence, while the actual exponent uses the cone-corrected finite-band rate realized by aligned trajectories rather than a large-deviation rate functional (Remark 5.6); its amplification is capped by logarithmic-norm contraction (Proposition 5.9). The hydrodynamic limit of Theorem 6.1 makes the branching-ratio terminology precise and ties macroscopic spectral instability to microscopic explosion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The theory provides a computable stability test for memory-coupled network dynamics: one forms the regime operator A z and kernel mass G z , computes the modal branching ratios ρ z,j = a z,j G z (or the spectral radius r ( A z ) G z in the nonnormal nonnegative case), and compares with the damping threshold c λ . The criterion is sharp under commutativity and topology-independent under scalar excitation, so the bifurcation can be located without simulating the full nonlocal dynamics, and the unstable modes are identified explicitly. The numerical study of Section 7 shows that this modal/spectral test can certify stability over a parameter range several times larger than a normbased bound.

The criterion is sharp under commutativity and topology-independent under scalar excitation, so the bifurcation can be located without simulating the full nonlocal dynamics, and the unstable modes are identified explicitly. The numerical study of Section 7 shows that this modal/spectral test can certify stability over a parameter range several times larger than a normbased bound.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The work unifies three classically separate objects into a single resolvent-family framework: the completely monotone Volterra resolvent calculus, the Hawkes branching ratio and its hydrodynamic limit, and non-normal transient growth measured by the logarithmic norm. The bridge is that modal branching ratios govern deterministic stability and microscopic branching explosion, while the logarithmic norm of a fixed lifted realization, rather than the spectral abscissa, provides the upper operator scale for pathwise finite-range amplification, with the realized cone correction determining the observed finite-band exponent. This places memory-driven heavy-burst phenomena and spectral stability within one logarithmic-norm/resolvent account.

The bridge is that modal branching ratios govern deterministic stability and microscopic branching explosion, while the logarithmic norm of a fixed lifted realization, rather than the spectral abscissa, provides the upper operator scale for pathwise finite-range amplification, with the realized cone correction determining the observed finite-band exponent. This places memory-driven heavy-burst phenomena and spectral stability within one logarithmic-norm/resolvent account.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The analysis is finite-dimensional ( X = R n × R n ); the infinite-dimensional node-space extension is natural (the constants are dimension-free) but requires sectorial-generator machinery and is left as future work. The finite-range amplification rests on the cone-alignment hypothesis Assumption 5, a positive-probability event whose sufficient conditions we give but do not derive from first principles for a general system. The control statement Proposition 5.9 is an idealized -feedback cap, not a robust control theorem. And the hydrodynamic martingale estimate requires g z ∈ L 2 loc , with the strongly singular fractional case ( α ≤ 1 2 ) handled only by regularization (Remark 6.2). Each of these is a concrete and tractable direction for sharpening the theory.

The control statement Proposition 5.9 is an idealized -feedback cap, not a robust control theorem. And the hydrodynamic martingale estimate requires g z ∈ L 2 loc , with the strongly singular fractional case ( α ≤ 1 2 ) handled only by regularization (Remark 6.2). Each of these is a concrete and tractable direction for sharpening the theory.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Further natural directions include sharp criticality analysis in the noncommuting case beyond the sufficient condition of Proposition 4.4, and central-limit refinements of Theorem 6.1 connecting to rough-volatility scaling limits [14].
